g.data-narration: Welcome to Deadlock Characterization. We will explore the four necessary conditions and resource allocation graphs.
A deadlock occurs when processes are blocked, each holding resources while waiting for others held by other processes.
g.text: A state where a set of processes are blocked because each process holds
a resource and waits for another resource acquired by another process.
Creates a circular wait - none can proceed!

g.data-narration: For deadlock to occur, four conditions must hold simultaneously: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait.
g.text: All four must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur

g.data-narration: Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource must be non-sharable. Only one process can use it at a time, like a printer.
g.text: At least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode.
Only ONE process can use it at a time. Others must wait.

g.data-narration: Hold and Wait: A process holds at least one resource while waiting for additional resources held by others.
g.text: A process must hold at least ONE resource and WAIT for MORE resources
that are currently held by other processes.
waits for R2 held by P2

g.data-narration: No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken. They are released only voluntarily after the process completes.
g.text: Resources cannot be preempted - cannot be forcibly taken away.
Only released VOLUNTARILY after the process completes its task.

g.data-narration: Circular Wait: A set of processes must form a cycle where each waits for a resource held by the next.
g.text: A set of waiting processes {P₀, P₁, ..., Pₙ} where:
P₀ waits for resource held by P₁
P₁ waits for resource held by P₂
... Pₙ waits for resource held by P₀

g.data-narration: The Resource-Allocation Graph models system state: processes as circles, resources as rectangles with instance dots.
g.text: A directed graph to visualize allocation and request state of the system.

g.data-narration: Request edge (P→R): Process requesting resource. Assignment edge (R→P): Resource allocated to process.
Key rules: No cycle = no deadlock. Cycle with single-instance resources = deadlock exists.
g.text: If graph has no cycles, deadlock cannot exist.
Cycle is necessary AND sufficient for single-instance resources.
⚠ Cycle + Multi-Instance = May Need Further Analysis
Cycle is necessary but NOT sufficient. Check if resources can be allocated to break cycle.

g.data-narration: With single-instance resources, a cycle confirms deadlock. With multiple instances, a cycle may not mean deadlock.
g.text: Cycle may NOT be deadlock if extra instance available

g.data-narration: Classic example: Process A holds disk, needs printer. Process B holds printer, needs disk. Both blocked forever!
This RAG shows P1 holds R1 requests R2; P2 holds R2 requests R1. The cycle confirms deadlock.
Three strategies: Prevention (ensure condition fails), Avoidance (check before allocation), Detection (allow and recover).
Exam tips: Memorize four conditions, practice drawing RAGs, look for cycles, cycle alone isn't enough for multi-instance.
Summary: Deadlock requires four conditions simultaneously. Use RAG to detect cycles. Apply prevention, avoidance, or detection strategies.
g.text: ✓ Four Coffman conditions → Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
✓ RAG: Cycle with single-instance = deadlock | Multi-instance = may need more analysis

g.data-narration: Practice with RAG examples to master deadlock detection. Understanding these concepts is essential for OS design.
g.text: Practice drawing RAGs | Remember: Cycle + single instance = deadlock guaranteed!
